peer_exemplars.reasoning: High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Very low openness, high authoritarian submission, and strong system justification drive skepticism toward forcing platform transparency, as it challenges existing power structures and feels like unnecessary interference.

Extreme authoritarianism (94.85%) and high conventionalism (96.59%) drive strong opposition to algorithmic transparency as a threat to orderly information control, reinforced by low trust in science and experts (21.73%) and moderate digital confidence.

High extraversion and very low agreeableness, combined with strong anti-domination values and moderate trust in science, produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque corporate power, but skepticism of state overreach tempers enthusiasm.

High openness and agreeableness, combined with strong fairness/cheating concerns (low) and high care/harm, lead to moderate support for transparency as a protective measure, but libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) tempers it.
High libertarian anti-censorship and GAL pluralism support transparency, but low openness and high conscientiousness limit enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates, yielding moderate support.

High conscientiousness and a strong sense of dutifulness drive a desire for predictable systems, but low libertarianism and authoritarian tendencies favor state-enforced oversight, yielding moderate support.
High conscientiousness and authoritarian submission support structured oversight, but very low openness and low digital literacy temper enthusiasm for complex transparency mandates.
